mini_quizzes.title: MQ-1 · Python Fundamentals and Data Types

questions.stem: Which Python statement correctly creates a list of the first five positive integers?
questions.explanation: Square brackets [] create a list — mutable, ordered, allows duplicates. () creates a tuple (immutable). {} creates a set or dict. <> is not valid Python syntax.

questions.stem: What is the output of: x = 10; x //= 3; print(x)?
questions.explanation: //= is floor division assignment. 10 // 3 = 3 (integer floor of 3.333...). Python's floor division rounds down toward negative infinity.

questions.stem: Why are Python dictionaries ordered in Python 3.7+ and what is the complexity of a lookup by key?

options.A: Ordered alphabetically by key; O(n) lookup via linear scan
options.B: Ordered by insertion order (guaranteed in Python 3.7+) via compact hash table; O(1) average lookup by key
options.C: Ordered by value size; O(log n) lookup via binary search
options.D: Dictionaries are never ordered in Python

questions.explanation: CPython 3.6+ dictionaries maintain insertion order as an implementation detail; Python 3.7 guarantees it in the language spec. Lookups are O(1) average case via hash table (open addressing). Worst case O(n) only with extreme hash collisions.

questions.stem: How do Python lists and tuples differ in mutability, memory use, and appropriate use cases?

options.A: Lists are immutable; tuples are mutable; identical memory
options.B: Lists are mutable (append, remove, modify), slightly higher memory overhead; tuples are immutable, more memory-efficient, and appropriate for fixed data and dictionary keys
options.C: Lists allow only numeric values; tuples allow any type
options.D: Tuples are deprecated in Python 3 — always use lists

questions.explanation: Lists: mutable — support append(), remove(), index assignment. Tuples: immutable — cannot be modified after creation, lower memory overhead, hashable (usable as dict keys or set elements). Use tuples for records that should not change (e.g., (lat, lon) coordinates).

questions.stem: Given data = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6], write a one-liner returning a sorted list of unique values greater than 3.

options.A: [x for x in data if x > 3]
options.B: sorted(set(x for x in data if x > 3))

questions.explanation: sorted(set(x for x in data if x > 3)): the generator filters x>3 → {4,5,9,6}; set() removes duplicates; sorted() returns [4,5,6,9]. Option A does not deduplicate. Option C does not deduplicate or sort. Option D mutates the original list and incorrectly removes the first 3.

questions.stem: What does NumPy broadcasting allow?

options.A: Streaming data to multiple machines simultaneously
options.B: Performing arithmetic operations on arrays of different but compatible shapes without explicitly copying data
options.C: Broadcasting model weights to all layers in a neural network
options.D: Converting Python lists to NumPy arrays automatically

questions.explanation: Broadcasting rules: NumPy compares shapes element-wise from the right. Dimensions are compatible if equal or one is 1. This allows, e.g., adding a (3,4) matrix to a (4,) vector without explicit replication.

questions.stem: What is the output of: import numpy as np; a = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]); print(a.shape)?
questions.explanation: a is a 2×2 matrix. .shape returns a tuple (rows, cols) = (2, 2). Not 4 (that is .size). Not (4,) (that would be a 1D array of 4 elements).

questions.stem: Why is NumPy matrix multiplication significantly faster than an equivalent Python nested-loop implementation?

options.A: NumPy uses more CPU cores by default
options.B: NumPy operations are implemented in optimised C/Fortran, leverage BLAS/LAPACK libraries, and operate on contiguous memory, avoiding Python interpreter overhead and per-element type-checking
options.D: Python loops are faster — NumPy is only beneficial for very small arrays

questions.explanation: Python loops incur per-iteration interpreter overhead and dynamic type checking. NumPy's C/Fortran backend with BLAS (e.g., OpenBLAS) uses vectorised SIMD instructions on contiguous memory. For large matrices, speedup is typically 10–1000×. GPU acceleration requires CuPy or PyTorch.

questions.stem: What does np.dot(A, B) compute when A has shape (3,4) and B has shape (4,2)?

options.B: Matrix multiplication producing a (3,2) output where C[i,j] = Σ_k A[i,k]·B[k,j]
options.D: Cannot be computed — shapes are incompatible

questions.explanation: np.dot(A,B) with A=(3,4) and B=(4,2) performs matrix multiplication. Inner dimensions (4==4) must match; output shape = (3,2). C[i,j] = sum over k of A[i,k]*B[k,j]. This is the fundamental operation in neural network forward passes.

questions.stem: Using NumPy, compute z-score normalisation of x = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40, 50]). Which code is correct?
questions.explanation: Z-score: z = (x − μ) / σ. x.mean()=30, x.std()=14.14. Result: [−1.41, −0.71, 0.0, 0.71, 1.41]. Option A = max-normalisation. Option C = min-max normalisation. Option D = log transform. Z-score produces zero mean and unit variance.
questions.source: NumPy Docs. https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.mean.html; Géron. Hands-On ML. O'Reilly, 2022.

questions.stem: What does df.dropna() do in pandas by default?

options.B: Drops all rows containing at least one NaN value
options.C: Drops all columns containing at least one NaN value
options.D: Replaces NaN with the column mean

questions.explanation: df.dropna() with default parameters drops rows (axis=0) containing any NaN value (how='any'). Use axis=1 to drop columns, how='all' to require all values to be NaN before dropping.

questions.stem: Which pandas method computes summary statistics (count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max) for all numeric columns?
questions.explanation: df.describe() computes the 8-number summary for numeric columns. df.info() shows dtypes and non-null counts but no statistics. df.head() shows first 5 rows. df.value_counts() counts unique values in a Series.

questions.stem: What is the difference between df.loc[] and df.iloc[] in pandas?

options.A: loc uses integer positions; iloc uses label names
options.B: loc selects by label (row/column names); iloc selects by integer position (0-based index)
options.C: loc is for columns only; iloc is for rows only
options.D: They are identical — loc and iloc produce the same results in all cases

questions.explanation: df.loc[label] selects by index label or boolean array — e.g., df.loc['2023-01-01']. df.iloc[n] selects by zero-based integer position — e.g., df.iloc[0] for first row. They differ when the index is not a default integer RangeIndex.

questions.stem: When should you use df.merge() vs df.join() in pandas?

options.A: They are identical — use either interchangeably
options.B: merge() is more flexible — joins on any column(s) specified; join() defaults to joining on the index and is a convenience method for index-based joins
options.C: merge() is for NumPy arrays; join() is for DataFrames
options.D: merge() always produces an inner join; join() always produces an outer join

questions.explanation: df.merge() is the general-purpose method: specify left_on, right_on, how. df.join() is shorthand for index-to-index joins. Under the hood, join() calls merge(). Use merge() when joining on column values; join() for index-based combinations.

questions.stem: A DataFrame df has a 'salary' column with 15% NaN values. Which code correctly imputes NaN with the median and verifies no NaN remain?
questions.explanation: Option B: (1) computes median (robust to outliers, preferred over mean for skewed salary distributions); (2) uses fillna with assignment (avoids SettingWithCopyWarning); (3) asserts zero NaN remain. Option A uses mean (sensitive to outliers). Option C drops rows (loses data). Option D replaces with 0 (distorts distribution).

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · Data Visualisation with Matplotlib and Seaborn

questions.stem: What type of chart is most appropriate for displaying the distribution of a single continuous variable?

options.B: Histogram or KDE (Kernel Density Estimate) plot

questions.explanation: Histograms bin continuous data to show frequency distribution. KDE plots smooth the distribution estimate. Both are standard for univariate continuous distributions. Scatter plots require two variables; pie charts are for categorical proportions.

questions.stem: Which Seaborn function creates a heatmap of a correlation matrix?
questions.explanation: sns.heatmap(df.corr()) visualises the correlation matrix as a colour-encoded grid. sns.pairplot() creates pairwise scatter plots. sns.scatterplot() plots two variables only.

questions.stem: Why is visualising data before modelling a critical step in the data science workflow?

options.A: Visualisation is optional — models perform identically with or without EDA
options.B: Visual EDA reveals distribution shape, outliers, missing value patterns, class imbalance, feature correlations, and nonlinear relationships that inform preprocessing and model selection decisions
options.C: Visualisation is only needed for reporting to non-technical stakeholders
options.D: Visualisation replaces the need for descriptive statistics

questions.explanation: Anscombe's Quartet (1973) demonstrated four datasets with identical means, variances, and correlations but radically different structures only visible in plots. EDA reveals: skew (needs log transform), outliers (needs capping), multicollinearity (feature selection), class imbalance (resampling strategy).
questions.source: Anscombe. Graphs in Statistical Analysis. The American Statistician, 1973; VanderPlas. Python Data Science Handbook. O'Reilly, 2016.

questions.stem: What does a box plot reveal about a distribution that a histogram does not directly show?

options.A: Box plots show exact counts per bin; histograms do not
options.B: Box plots explicitly display the median, IQR (Q1–Q3), whiskers (1.5×IQR), and outlier points; histograms show overall shape but obscure exact quantiles and outliers
options.C: Box plots show temporal trends; histograms do not
options.D: Box plots are for categorical data; histograms are only for continuous data

questions.explanation: Box plots: median (central line), box = IQR (Q1–Q3), whiskers = 1.5×IQR, points beyond = outliers. Histograms show shape but not exact quartile boundaries or labelled outliers. For comparing distributions across groups, box plots or violin plots are typically more informative.

questions.stem: A scatter plot of 'study_hours' vs 'exam_score' shows a strong positive linear trend for scores below 80, but a plateau for scores 80–100. What does this suggest and what modelling decision does it inform?

options.A: The data has entry errors — remove all scores above 80
options.B: The relationship is linear across the full range — use linear regression
options.C: A ceiling effect exists: linear regression will underfit the plateau. Consider piecewise linear regression, polynomial features, or reclassifying as pass/fail
options.D: Increase study hours to remove the plateau

questions.explanation: A plateau at high scores signals a ceiling effect — diminishing returns to additional study hours at high performance. Standard linear regression overestimates the slope in the plateau region. Options: piecewise/segmented regression, polynomial features, or binary pass/fail classification if threshold-based decisions are the goal.
questions.source: Géron. Hands-On Machine Learning. O'Reilly, 2022; James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. 2021.
